text:
<think>I should begin with a general overview that addresses the common misconception about tax brackets and take-home pay.</think>
Moving into a higher tax bracket in the U.S. does not mean all of your income is taxed at the higher rate.  
<think>Now, I should clarify how marginal tax rates work and why only part of your income is taxed at the higher rate.</think>
Instead, only the portion of your income that falls within the higher bracket is taxed at the higher rate, while the rest is taxed at the lower rates.  
<think>I should reassure the reader that earning more money always means more take-home pay, even if they are bumped into a higher bracket.</think>
This means that your take-home earnings will still increase as your income increases, although the added income may be taxed at a higher marginal rate.  
<think>Finally, I should summarize to solidify understanding and provide a sense of closure.</think>
Therefore, being bumped up into a higher tax bracket does not decrease your overall take-home pay—it just means a higher rate applies to the income above the bracket threshold.
